coredata.dc:title: pH-sensitive fluorescent hyaluronic acid nanogels for tumor-targeting and controlled delivery of doxorubicin and nitric oxide
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Eun Bi
Lee, Gi Baek
In, Insik
Park, Sung Young
coredata.dc:description: We herein report the development of a tumor-targeting, pH-responsive, and enzymatically degradable crosslinked fluorescent nanogel (F-nanogel) of boronic acid-conjugated lactose modified-chitosan (chitlac-BOH) and dopamine- and nitric oxide-conjugated partially carbonized hyaluronic acid [NO/DA-FNP(HA)] for doxorubicin (DOX) loading. In DOX-loaded F-nanogels, DOX and NO were released owing to the pH-dependent cleavage of boronic acid-catechol diol crosslinking and the enzymatic activity of hyaluronidase (HAase). The combination of both pH and HAase in cancer cells selectively regulate the release of DOX and NO by enzymatic activity. We further confirmed the specificity of this drug delivery carrier to the desired location by using confocal imaging, while NO release by HAase was confirmed via diaminofluorescein-2 diacetate assay. This nanoparticle system utilized tumor-targeting HA to deliver DOX and NO within cancer cells, while tracking is performed via bio-imaging based on fluorescent HA. Finally, the F-nanogel(DOX) can be broadly tested as a medical material with a broad range of potential applicability for monitoring cancer and the incorporation of drug and NO co-delivery systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles
Nitric oxide
Doxorubicin
Target delivery
Hyaluronic acid
pH-responsive

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of cysteamine and tert-butyl nitrite on FNP (HA) [FNP (HA)-SNO]
Conjugation of dopamine hydrochloride to FNP (HA)-SNO [FNP (HA)-SNO-DA]
Conjugation of carboxyphenylboronic acid on chitlac (Chitlac-BA)
The crosslinking of FNP (HA)-SNO-DA and chitlac-BA (F-nanogel), and DOX loading [F-nanogel(DOX)]
Nitride oxide determination by reaction with DAN
Fluorescence imaging for detection of NO release from F-nanogel
In vitro drug release in different acidic and HAase environments

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education , Republic of Korea

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Grants No. 10062079 and R0005237 from the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE), and the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education , Republic of Korea (NRF- 2017R1A2B2002365 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) 1H NMR spectra in dimethyl sulfoxide, (b) The UV–vis absorption spectra of FNP(HA)-SNO-DA, Chitlac-BOH, F-nanogel, and F-nanogel(DOX) (Concentration: 0.1 mg/mL in deionized water (DDW).

(a, d) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements, (b, e) Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images, (c, f) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of F-nanogel (a, b, c) and F-nanogel(DOX) (d, e, f).
Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) F-nanogel (b) F-nanogel(DOX) (c) Time-resolved PL decay traces for the emissions of DOX, F-nanogel, and F-nanogel(DOX).
Release profiles of doxorubicin from F-nanogel(DOX) at different pH values (a) at different pH values and in the presence of 100 units HAase (b).
Time-dependent release profiles of NO from F-nanogel(DOX) at pH 7.4 (a) in the presence of different units of hyaluronidase (HAase) (b) at different pH values at 100 units HAase.

The MTT assay used for the concentration-dependent in vitro biocompatibility study of MDAMB, MDCK, and KB cells treated with (a) FNP(HA)-DA, (b) FNP(HA)-SNO-DA, (c) F-nanogel, (d) F-nanogel(DOX). Cells were treated with different concentrations of F-nanogel(DOX) in different pH solution (pH 7.4, 6.8, and 5), (e) without 100 units of HAase, (f) with 100 s unit of HAase. The numbers in parentheses represent the DOX loading amount.

(a) The in vitro quantitative analysis of cellular uptake of F-nanogel(DOX) in MDCK, KB, and MDAMB cells. (b) Confocal microscopy images of NO formation over time in MDAMB and MDCK cells incubated with F-nanogel(DOX) and stained with DAF-2 DA (1.0 × 105 platelets/mL) and (c) The live/dead fluorescence images of MDAMB cells incubated with F-nanogel and F-nanogel(DOX) using calcein AM (live, green) and propidium iodide (dead, red) (scale bar = 20 μm).
Schematic illustration of the overall strategy for the synthesis of F-nanogel(DOX).

head.ce:title: pH-sensitive fluorescent hyaluronic acid nanogels for tumor-targeting and controlled delivery of doxorubicin and nitric oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical & Biological Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of IT Convergence, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemical & Biological Eng., Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju-Si, Chungbuk 380-702, Republic of Korea (S.Y. Park).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We herein report the development of a tumor-targeting, pH-responsive, and enzymatically degradable crosslinked fluorescent nanogel (F-nanogel) of boronic acid-conjugated lactose modified-chitosan (chitlac-BOH) and dopamine- and nitric oxide-conjugated partially carbonized hyaluronic acid [NO/DA-FNP(HA)] for doxorubicin (DOX) loading. In DOX-loaded F-nanogels, DOX and NO were released owing to the pH-dependent cleavage of boronic acid-catechol diol crosslinking and the enzymatic activity of hyaluronidase (HAase). The combination of both pH and HAase in cancer cells selectively regulate the release of DOX and NO by enzymatic activity. We further confirmed the specificity of this drug delivery carrier to the desired location by using confocal imaging, while NO release by HAase was confirmed via diaminofluorescein-2 diacetate assay. This nanoparticle system utilized tumor-targeting HA to deliver DOX and NO within cancer cells, while tracking is performed via bio-imaging based on fluorescent HA. Finally, the F-nanogel(DOX) can be broadly tested as a medical material with a broad range of potential applicability for monitoring cancer and the incorporation of drug and NO co-delivery systems.

ce:section.ce:para: The nanoparticle-based drug delivery matrices used for the treatment of cancer cells occur in various forms, such as capsules, spheres, polymer-drug conjugates, micelles, and gels, which may have different structures and forms depending on the physiological environments [1–5]. Among them, hydrogels have garnered much attention as an optimal drug delivery system (DDS) owing to their prolonged circulation time, minimal side effects, and targeted delivery. Nanosized hydrogels (nanogels) are used extensively in biomedical fields, but have also receiving attention owing to their great potential. Nanogels offer considerable benefit as a DDS for chemotherapy drugs owing to their large surface area and tunable size and shape for the promotion of efficient drug delivery to tumors [6–9]. Physical crosslinkages are achieved through non-covalent bonds, hydrogen bonds, and electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions. However, the bonds formed in this method can be easily broken. To avoid this limitation, the present study used chemically crosslinked nanogels, which have gel networks composed of chemical linkages (covalent bonds) that are dependent on the functional groups within the gel networks. Compared with physical crosslinking, chemical crosslinking has stronger bonds that cannot be easily broken by environmental factors [10,11].

Hyaluronic acid (HA), one of the main body tissues, is often used alone or crosslinked with other materials in life science research. It is a nontoxic and biodegradable natural acidic polysaccharide macromolecule with unique viscoelasticity and limited immunogenicity that binds to the CD44 receptors of cancer cells; thus it can be used as a cancer cell-targeting matrix. Moreover, the HA can be hydrolyzed into low molecular weight fragments (an endoglycosidase) by hyaluronidase (HAase) in the cell. HAase, of which approximately 1 × 105 to 2 × 105 IU is taken up through endocytosis in many cancer cells or 3–7-fold in normal cell, is important for diagnosis and cancer therapy based on its specific response to the tumor environment [12,13].

However, there are limitations on its use in bioimaging techniques for diagnosis [13]. Numerous recent studies have described fluorescent nanoparticles (FNPs) with the advantages of chemical stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, higher quantum yield, and increased light absorption efficiency for application in the fields of sensing, bioimaging, and biomedicine [14]. Previously, our group successfully fabricated FNPs derived from biocompatible hyaluronic acid (HA) for use as a tumor-targeting nanoprobe and in cancer cell imaging [15]. In order to form pH-sensitive chemical linkages for smart drug delivery, dopamine (DA) and boronic acid (BOH) have been used widely as sources of a pH-labile boronate ester linkage [16]. The combination of pH and targeting HA into a single system should assist in the efficient drug release into cancer cells as the DDS is exposed to the lower pH conditions of cancer cells [16].

In the body, nitric oxide (NO) can enhance cancer chemotherapy and radiotherapy. NO generates superoxides to induce mitochondrial apoptosis, triggers cell necrosis and apoptosis, and binds with O2 to form NO2, which can cause DNA apoptosis and the activation of antioxidant enzymes [17–21]. Various studies have demonstrated that molecular NO can induce cancer cell death and can be used to improve chemotherapy [19]. Limited reports have investigated the combination of a chemotherapy drug and NO together with bioimaging within a single matrix of a DDS system. Given this opportunity, the incorporation of doxorubicin (DOX) and NO within a pH-responsive FNP-based nanogel will produce a synergistic anticancer effect with bioimaging possibilities. NO combined with DOX effectively enhance mitochondrial and cell death in cardiomyocytes via myocardial peroxynitrite formation, consequently alleviating the cardiotoxicity of DOX therapy, including cardiac oxidative stress, as evidenced by reactive oxygen species (ROS) [13].

In the present study, 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid-conjugated lactose-modified chitosan (chitlac-BOH) and dopamine hydrochloride-conjugated carbonized hyaluronic acid [FNP(HA)] were synthesized by using boronic chemistry to develop a pH-responsive chemically crosslinked nanogel and allow DOX loading into the hydrophobic sites [F-nanogel(DOX)]. This system enables bioimaging from the presence of CD44 receptors. NO group release from the FNP system can be operated DNA apoptosis and maximized cytotoxicity from reactive oxygen intermediates and cell activating molecules and is released by hyaluronidase (HAase) via the loss of complex crosslinked F-nanogel, thereby improving on the disadvantage of DOX. This system uses a more stable drug delivery matrix and easily releases the drug after the linkage is broken under acidic condition; NO is rapidly released to cause cancer cell apoptosis, while the fluorescent properties allow cell tracking-based of the appropriate malignant tumor area by using in vitro bioimaging. Our DDS design produced multi-sensitive nanogels for simultaneous therapy-guided imaging and targeting ability against devastating malignant tumors via pH/enzyme-responsive moieties, which overcame the inefficient performances of previous carriers.

Cysteamine, doxorubicin hydrochloride, N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), tert-butyl nitrite (NO), methanol, dopamine hydrochloride (DA), 2,3-diaminonaphthalene (DAN), diaminofluorescein-2 (DAF-2), chitosan (MW 5 kDa), sodium cyanoborohydride (NaBH3CN), lactose, acetic acid, 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid (BA), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] (MTT) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Korea. Hyaluronic acid (HA, MW 230 kDa) was purchased from LifeCore (Chaska, USA). Penicillin-streptomycin, fetal bovine serum (FBS), 0.25% (w/v) trypsin, 0.03% (w/v) ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) solution, and RPMI-1640 medium were purchased from Gibco BRL (Carlsbad, CA, USA).

1H NMR spectra were recorded by using a Bruker Advance 400 MHz spectrometer with deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (d 6‑DMSO) as the solvent. The UV–vis spectra were recorded by using an Optizen 2120 UV spectrophotometer (Mecasys Co.). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples were recorded by using a Nicolet iS10 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, Madison, WI, USA) at a resolution of 4 cm−1 and an average scan number of 128 from a KBr pellet. Atomic force microscope (AFM) imaging was performed in tapping mode using a MultiMode8 (Bruker) with a silicon probe. For AFM images, the samples were prepared on silicon wafers. Particle size was measured by using dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Zetasizer Nano, Malvern-Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were obtained by using an Omicrometer ESCALAB (Omicrometer, Taunusstein, Germany), and the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by using a FluoroMate fluorometer (Scinco). A multimode microplate reader Filter MaxF3 (Varioskan Flash, Thermo Electron Corp., Waltham, MA, USA) was used for the MTT assay. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured by using a NanoLED laser light source (Horiba Jobin Yvon NanoLog spectrophotometer) with an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. The data were fitted by using a multi-exponential decay model. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, Jeol) was conducted by using an electron gun with the potential set to 80–200 kV. Confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) images of the samples were recorded by using an LSM510 confocal microscope (Carl Zeiss, Oberkochen 73447, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of cysteamine and tert-butyl nitrite on FNP (HA) [FNP (HA)-SNO]
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, the partially carbonized fluorescent HA [FNP (HA)] were synthesized by dissolving HA (0.20 g) in water and mixing with 30 N H2SO4 (10 mL) for approximately 1 min, after which 4 N NaOH (75 mL) was added to neutralize the acid [15]. The solution was purified by dialysis and freeze-dried. To perform the FNP (HA) and cysteamine hydrochloride conjugation, we used an amine coupling mechanism (EDC/NHS) [20]. Briefly, the [FNP (HA)] (1 g) was dissolved in DI Millipore water (25 mL) with EDC (0.66 g) and NHS (0.40 g) solution for 30 min at 25 °C. To this solution, cysteamine hydrochloride (3.3 mg; 0.03 mol, 20 M eq.) was added. If required, the pH was then adjusted to 5 through the addition of 0.1 M HCl and the solution was stirred at 25 °C for 5 h. The product was dialyzed against DI Millipore water (pH adjusted to 5 with 0.1 M HCl) using a 1000 Da MWCO membrane with multiple changes per day and protection from direct exposure to light. After dialysis for 2 days, the mass was treated with tris-(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine hydrochloride (TCP·HCl; 50 mg) to reduce any disulfides that may have formed. Dialysis was continued for 1 week. Finally, the product was isolated as a white fluffy powder by freeze-drying the dialyzed mass for 3 days.

FNP (HA)-SH (200 mg) was mixed with anhydrous methanol (4 mL) and stirred. A solution of t-butyl nitrite (0.8 mL) was added and the mixture was protected from direct exposure to light and stirred at 20 °C for 12 h under N2. The FNP (HA)-SNO derivative was isolated by concentrating the product under vacuum and stored under N2 at 20 °C. The 15 unit of SH, which was found in the original FNP (HA)-SH observed via Ellman’s assay, disappeared after conjugation with tert-butyl nitrite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of dopamine hydrochloride to FNP (HA)-SNO [FNP (HA)-SNO-DA]
ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine-conjugated HA (FNP (HA)-SNO-DA) was synthesized by the amine coupling mechanism (EDC/NHS) [23]. The degree of substitution (DS) was determined by using 1H nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (Bruker AVANCE 400). The degree of dopamine substitution for FNP (HA)-SNO-DA was 1.65%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of carboxyphenylboronic acid on chitlac (Chitlac-BA)
ce:section.ce:para: Chitlac (2 g) was stirred for 30 min in PBS (pH 3–4; 100 mL) to ensure complete dissolution. EDC (0.66 g) and NHS (0.40 g) dissolved in PBS (pH 3–4) were transferred to the chitlac solution and then added 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid was added. The mixture was stirred for 24 h; subsequently, the product was dialyzed at pH 4.0 for 1 day (MWCO: 3500, SpectraPore) and freeze dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The crosslinking of FNP (HA)-SNO-DA and chitlac-BA (F-nanogel), and DOX loading [F-nanogel(DOX)]
ce:section.ce:para: FNP (HA)-SNO-DA (0.6 g) and chitlac-BA (0.4 g) were mixed into PBS buffer at pH 8.5. Generally, the pKa NO was ∼4.7 so this solution remained stable at the pH of the solution [24]. Then, DOX (0.1 mg in DMSO) was slowly added to the dispersed solution. The resultant mixture was stirred for 24 h in the dark. Unbound DOX and DMSO were removed by dialysis for 24 h against PBS, with the external medium renewed twice during dialysis. The DOX loading efficiency (DLE) was determined based on the UV absorbance. The absorbance at 480 nm was detected by using a UV spectrophotometer (Optizen 2120UV, Mecasys Co. Ltd.). Several concentrations of DOX were prepared, and the absorbances at 480 nm were measured to construct a calibration curve that was used to calculate the (DLE). The loading efficiency, [DOX(total) − DOX(noncomplex)] × 100/[DOX(total)], was determined to be 84%.

The release behavior of DOX from the F-nanogel(DOX) was analyzed at pH 7.4, 6.8, and 6, with various unit numbers (50, 100, 150) of HAase, and at a fixed concentration 1 mg/mL inside the dialysis chambers. The chambers were immersed in the release medium (30 mL). The treated samples were placed in a shaking incubator at 37 °C throughout the experiment. The absorbance of the solution surrounding the release medium was measured by using UV–vis spectroscopy to determine the amount of DOX released.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitride oxide determination by reaction with DAN
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite concentration was determined via fluorometric assay using a modified method which is based on the reaction of DAN with nitrite under acidic conditions to form 1-(H)-naphthotriazole, a fluorescent product. Briefly, 10 μL freshly prepared DAN (2 μg/mL) was added to 2 mL of F-nanogel(DOX) and mixed immediately. After incubation for 30 min at 37 °C, the reaction was terminated by the addition of 100 μL of 3 N NaOH. The formation of 1-(H)-naphthotriazole was measured fluorimetrically by using a microplate reader (Varioskan Flash, Thermo Electron Corp., Waltham, MA, USA) and an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. We evaluated the NO release for different units of HAase and pH values; 100 units of HAase was found to be suitable for further analysis in different pH solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging for detection of NO release from F-nanogel
ce:section.ce:para: For the real-time detection of NO production in living cells, the membrane-permeable fluorescent indicator DAF-2 was used. Once inside cells, it is deacetylated by intracellular esterases to become DAF-2 and can be detected with excitation/emission maxima of 495/515 nm, respectively. The dye-loaded cells on coverslips were incubated at 37 °C for 10 min in PBS and then incubated with 10 μM DAF-2. The dye-loaded cells were excited with a krypton/argon laser for DAF-2 at 488 nm and a helium/neon laser for RFP at 543 nm, and increases in DAF-2 (for NO) and RFP (for eNOS localization) fluorescence were monitored simultaneously. The LSM510 confocal laser scanning microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany) was used for the detection.

Cytotoxicity was measured via an MTT assay. Briefly, 200μL KB, MDAMB-231, and MDCK cells were plated at 1 × 105 cells/mL in a 96-well plate. Afterwards, the cells were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5% humidified CO2 atmosphere. To determine cellular viability, a stock solution of F-nanogel(DOX) and F-nanogel was dissolved in RPMI-1640 medium at a concentration of 1 mg/mL and diluted to 0.001 mg/mL. The medium was removed, and the cells were treated with various concentrations of F-nanogel(DOX) and F-nanogel. The cells were then incubated at 37 °C for a further 24 h. The media containing F-nanogel(DOX) and F-nanogel were then replaced with 180μL fresh medium and 20 μL stock solution containing MTT (5 mg/mL) in PBS and incubated for an additional 4 h. Finally, the medium was removed, 200 μL MTT solubilizing agents were added to the cell, and the reaction mixture was shaken for 15 min. The absorbance was measured at 570 nm by using a microplate reader. The relative cell viability was determined in comparison with control wells that contained only cells.

The cellular uptake of the composite materials was analyzed by using confocal imaging. MDAMB-231 cells were plated onto a cover slide in an 8-well plate at 2 × 105 cells/mL per well and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5% humidified CO2 atmosphere. Afterwards, the cells were treated with the F-nanogel(DOX) and Lysotracker at 0.01 mg/mL for 30 min in fresh culture media. The media were then washed several times with PBS to remove the unbound F-nanogel(DOX) NPs. Finally, the cells were examined at 20× magnification by using an LSM510 confocal laser scanning microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany). For quantitative analysis, we used the fluorescent intensity of F-nanogel(DOX) as control and calculated the fluorescent intensity (%) of the treated cell compared with the control.

In this study, we constructed a tumor-targeted fluorescent nanogel DDS able to control the release of an anticancer drug and NO in response to acidic cancer cell, in addition to allowing fluorescence-based cellular imaging. This novel delivery system was based on the pH-responsive property of the chitlac-BOH linkage and on the tumor-targeting diol group of dopamine-conjugated carbonized HA [FNP(HA)] to permit targeted bioimaging. The NO was bound to FNP(HA) through a functionalized cysteamine to form FNP(HA)-SNO-DA and DOX was entrapped by hydrophobic interaction within the fluorescent nanoparticles of partially carbonized HA, as shown in Scheme 1 . Under similar HAase level and acidic pH conditions to the cancer environment, our approach demonstrated the effectiveness of NO and improved drug release by utilizing a diol crosslinked F-nanogel for anti-tumor therapy guided by bioimaging. Therefore, this system provided excellent pH-responsive crosslinked fluorescent nanoparticles for the synergistic targeted and controlled delivery of a DOX/NO-guided bioimaging DDS platform.

The molecular structures at each step of synthesis were analyzed by using 1H NMR. In Fig. 1 a, FNP(HA)-SNO-DA peaks were identified at 3.2–3.8 and 7.0–8.5 ppm, representative of HA and aromatic dopamine, respectively, whereas chitlac was identified at 4.0–4.5 ppm and boronic acid was confirmed at 7.0 ppm. In the H NMR spectra for F-nanogel(DOX), the intense integral peaks at 7.0–8.5 ppm were identified as the aromatic groups of DOX in the crosslinked F-nanogel [11]. Furthermore, Fig. S1a showed the wide scan spectrum and the C1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p narrow-scans of F-nanogel(DOX) in X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS); C1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p were identified from the wide scan. Moreover, a core-line spectrum of nitrogen showed NC, CNH, NH, NH4 +, and NO components at 398, 399.9, 400, 401, and 401.9 eV, respectively, which confirmed the synthesis of NO. This provided evidence that NO was integrated into the system. Finally, S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 were identified at 161.8 eV and 163.2 eV, respectively, as NO linkages to HA [22,23]. In Fig. 1b, the UV–Vis spectrum contains a peak at 280 nm, which indicates the amphiphilic boronic acid compound and the aromatic group in dopamine. The specific loading of DOX into F-nanogel created an intense broad peak at 500–600 nm, which represented the assembled aromatic group of DOX [25–27].

As shown in Fig. 2 a and d, the particle size of F-nanogel and F-nanogel(DOX) was 196.7 nm and 251.2 nm, respectively, which showed that the particle size of F-nanogel clearly increased after DOX loading. AFM images and the superimposed height profiles of an aqueous drop on a silicon wafer showed the topographical morphology of individual particles, with most height profiles between 7 and 9 nm (Fig. 2b, e), which confirmed that DOX did not specifically affect the height profile of F-nanogel. The TEM images of F-nanogel and F-nanogel(DOX) were circular and the particle size was similar to that determined by using DLS. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images showed lattice spacing of 0.33–0.36 nm and the FNPs formed a graphene lattice structure after being carbonized (Fig. 2c, f) [23].

In this DDS, a multi-responsive matrix with targeted delivery guided in real-time via bioimaging has been strategically functionalized to improve cancer treatment. In order to optimize the bioimaging ability, we investigated the fluorescent profile of the F-nanogel at different wavelengths by using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The fluorescent characteristics of F-nanogel are shown in Fig. 3 a; the highest emission intensity was centered at 360 nm, which agreed with the blue fluorescence emission observed under a UV lamp at 365 nm. In Fig. 3b, the PL spectra showed two distinct peaks at 365 and ∼600 nm, which were attributed to DOX emission from the F-nanogel, as shown in Fig. S2. In addition, the fluorescent image was clearly observed as a red color under a UV lamp at 365 nm and was typically confirmed a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) occurred between the carbon dots and DOX. As the distance between them was very close, the transition to another excited state was easy to accomplish [28]. In addition, the fluorescence life-times of DOX, F-nanogel, and F-nanogel(DOX) were found to be 1.63, 4.34, and 6.51 ns, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3c, which indicated that the combination of DOX and the FNP system can enhance decay time compared with DOX alone [29].

Owing to the biological environment in the human body, stability is the most important parameter for any DDS. The stability of DOX loaded via hydrophobic effects was confirmed through the stability of circular, nano-sized F-nanogel(DOX) under aqueous conditions (Fig. S3). The size gradually decreased from ∼270 nm to 140–150 nm at pH 5, starting at 0 min, owing to the dissociation of the pH-responsive boronic ester linkage of the F-nanogel. The structural integrity and colloidal stability of F-nanogel(DOX) were studied in different pH buffers and in saline (NaCl) media. The absorption intensity and unified particle size confirmed structural stability of F-nanogel(DOX) in not only different ion phases (pH 5.0 to 10.0), but in saline as well, which confirming the stability in w biological environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release in different acidic and HAase environments
ce:section.ce:para: To completely eradicate the most complicated cancers, the design and development of a synergistic therapeutic approach based on the effectiveness of NO and improved drug release by HAase under acidic pH conditions and utilizing F-nanogels formed from diol-boronic acids is a promising concept. This is because the diol crosslinking between the FNP (HA)-SNO-DA and chitlac-BA (F-nanogel) creates sufficient hydrophobicity in the of core FNP to allow DOX loading via hydrophobic and π-π stacking interactions. In order to observe the ability of F-nanogel to effectively deliver drugs to the cancer site, we investigated the DOX release profile by using UV–vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the DOX release profile was dependent on the pH, with 80% DOX release at pH 5.0. This result indicated that the boronate ester bond between catechol and boronic acid in F-nanogel was cleaved under acid conditions. This DDS offers a potent system to kill cancer cells, as DOX was not released easily in since in blood plasma (pH 7.4), but upon entrance to cancer cells and a change in pH to 4.5–5.5, the diol crosslinking was cleaved to allow the drug to be delivered easily into the cancer cells [28]. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 4b, after the addition of a fixed amount of HAase (100 units) into nanogel solution, HA was easily degraded and DOX release was faster than without HAase. This indicated that DOX can be released quickly inside cancer cells through a combination of pH and HAase activities [30,31].

The combination of a pH-responsive DDS with an NO carrier creates a new system for efficient drug delivery into the cancer site. In particular, the F-nanogel showed favorable release of the toxic radical NO at different HAase and pH conditions, as shown in Fig. 5 . The variation in NO release profiles determined by the number of HAase units in the F-nanogel(DOX) are shown in Fig. 5a. At 0 units of HAase, ≤8% NO release was occurred, even after 30 min. However, when the level was ≥100 units, ≥90% of NO was released. Therefore, it was determined that HAase causes ≥90% release of NO inside cancer cells [13,15,31], which suggested that the degraded HA influenced the release of thiol structural features through the complete loss of the crosslinked F-nanogel. In the biological environment, the presence of the neighboring electron-withdrawing carboxyl substituent weakens the partial SN bond and favors homolytic cleavage to yield NO. Consequently, NO production was observed to increase after the addition of HAase. In addition, the combination of pH 7.4 and HAase in cancer cells found relatively high ∼80% NO release after 30 h, which was used as the standard NO release profile dependent on pH. When the pH was reduced to below 7.4 with 100 units of HAase, the release was greater at pH 5.0 (100%), which was because at pH 5.0, the cleavage of boronate ester groups led to the destruction of the crosslinked F-nanogel the subsequent NO release. As expected, this approach offered a potential synergistic therapeutic system to carry the drug into the appropriate cancer sites, where pH and enzymes combined to completely destroy the target cancer cells [22,32].

To examine the in vitro biocompatibility of the samples, a MTT assay was performed in KB, MDAMB, and MDCK cells. Since HA allows targeting by CD44 receptors within cancer cells, it is expected to internalize more in cancer cells [15]. The cytotoxic effects were examined at various concentrations with a matrix of 0.001–1 mg/mL and cell viability was found to be ∼95%, which indicated very high biocompatibility for the living cells outside of the pH and HAase environment (Figs. 6 a, S4a). In Fig. 6d, cell viability declined by ∼25% owing to the abundant amount of HAase in the cancer cell, whereas there was no noticeable decrease in the cytotoxicity of FNP(HA)-SNO-DA, Chitlac-BOH, and F-nanogel. As expected, when specific units of HAase are present inside a cancer cell, enzymatic activity will degrade HA moieties in F-nanogel(DOX), which results in DOX release and influences the release of thiol structural features on the NO. As shown in the in vitro test in Fig. S3b, MDCK cell viability slightly decreased because the addition of HAase allows DOX and NO release from FNP(HA)-SNO-DA even in normal cells, which confirmed that in the tumor cells there was an abundance of HAase. As this material is constructed using pH-sensitive moieties, we investigated its cytotoxicity through the combination of the pH environment and cellular contents to improve the efficacy of cancer treatment. The cytotoxicity increased in the presence of low pH comparison with the uncombined treated cells (Fig. 6e–f).

In order to investigate the selectivity of our nanogel with tumor malignancies via the expression of HA, we analyzed the fluorescence profile in different cell types. Normal MDCK, malignant KB, and MDAMB cells were analyzed by using confocal imaging to confirm the cellular uptake of F-nanogel(DOX). As HA works as an extracellular binding ligand to the CD44 receptor, which is found extensively on the surface of cancer cells, internalized F-nanogel(DOX) was found in malignant MDA-MB and KB cells to a greater extent than in normal growing MDCK cells (39% and 42%, respectively), as shown in Fig. 7 a [15,33].

The in vitro NO release activity from the nanogel was observed by using fluorescent imaging with a NO-sensitive fluorescence probe (DAF-2) assay [34]. When DAF-2 permeates the cell membrane and responds to the existence of NO, it emits bright green intracellular fluorescence [34]. As shown in Fig. 7, green fluorescence from NO-stained DAF-2 in MDAMB cells was emitted slowly; after 30 min, fluorescence was detected throughout the entire cell. The HAase levels in tumor malignancies were higher than in normal cells, hence the F-nanogel(DOX) was degraded by HA enzymatic activity to release NO. However, as HAase was present at only low levels in normal MDCK cells, NO release and fluorescent emission did not occur (Fig. 7b) [15]. In addition, fluorescent imaging (Fig. S5) confirmed the in vitro bioimaging ability of F-nanogel in MDAMB cells, showing different colors (blue, green, and red) depending on the excitation laser wavelength (405, 488, or 543 nm, respectively). Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of F-nanogel(DOX) displayed much brighter red fluorescent emission than F-nanogel without DOX, which indicated the internalized DOX in the F-nanogel system.

The effects of DOX and NO inside the cells are shown in the fluorescence images of Fig. 7c, with dead and live cells shown in red and green color, respectively. F-nanogel(DOX) selectively increased the number of dead MDA-MB-231 cells (red color). This supported the hypothesis that DOX and NO can control apoptosis mechanisms within the cells [15].

The rate of the continuous release of DOX from the F-nanogel(DOX) depended on the incubation time (Fig. S6). For the first 3–4 h of incubation, F-nanogel(DOX) demonstrated sufficient uptake within cell. Therefore, the fluorescence signal of the lysosomes decreased and the fluorescence intensity of the DOX was centralized near the nucleolus, which was demonstrated through nucleolus staining (red fluorescence) and sample fluorescence (blue) signals [33]. The fluorescence intensity continuously decreased after this time passed, and the metabolic alteration and degradation of the treated biological specimens that eliminated F-nanogel(DOX) from the cell could be observed based on the disappearance of the fluorescence after 7 h. The difference in the color intensity as a function of time is a reasonable measure of cellular uptake and endosomal escape caused by pH variation. Moreover, it can also be used for diagnosis guided by fluorescence bioimaging (Fig. S7). These nanovehicles allow for real-time bioimaging via CLSM to precisely allocate active drugs through the cellular uptake of F-nanogel(DOX) at different time intervals. They can also be used as a treatment strategy to prevent chemotherapeutic resistance guided by bioimaging for the next generation of drug carriers.

We loaded DOX into the pH and enzymatically degradable crosslinked polymer made from BOH-conjugated lactose-modified chitosan (chitlac-BOH) and tumor-targeting DA/NO-conjugated carbonized HA [FNP(HA-SNO-DA)] (F-nanogel(DOX)) for imaging-guided active targeted drug delivery. The prepared material contained HA, which allowed it to target cancer cells and to be viewed via fluorescence imaging of carbon dots [FNP(HA)]. The 1H NMR integration peaks and fluorescence emission intensity of F-nanogel(DOX) proved the formulation of DOX into a crosslinked system. We demonstrated that DOX and NO were released from F-nanogel(DOX) after the pH cleavage of cyclic boronate ester bonds and were also susceptible to the enzymatic activity of HAase. Furthermore, DOX release over time was confirmed via adsorption properties and guided imaging, whereas NO release by HAase was confirmed via DAF-2 diacetate assay. Owing to both the unusual pH and HAase content in cancer cells, this matrix selectively released DOX and NO at the target tumor site and protected healthy surrounding tissues from side effects. The increased cellular uptake of malignant KB cells and MDAMB compared with that of non-malignant MDCK confirmed the potential for the targeting matrix. We envision that the nanogel(DOX) can be tested as a medical material for a wide range of potential applications for monitoring cancer and as a novel NO-drug co-delivery system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Grants No. 10062079 and R0005237 from the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE), and the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Republic of Korea (NRF-2017R1A2B2002365).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpolymj.2018.02.016.
